source_metadata.title_raw: Jeremy Brown - A-19-212 - November 19, 2019 - Burnt Ranch

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 19, 2019

Jeremy Brown
P.O. Box 253
Burnt Ranch, Ca 95573

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-212

Dear Mr. Brown:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

As a member of the Trinity County Board of Supervisors and the sole member of the limited liability company established for your cannabis grower business, may you participate in the following decisions:

1. An application to rezone four highway commercial parcels to allow a commercial cannabis storage and distribution facility.

2. The draft Environmental Impact Report specific to the County Cannabis Program ("Cannabis-EIR")

3. A cannabis tax initiative approval before the Board applicable to cultivation, retail, distribution and manufacturing.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-212
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

1. Yes. The rezone application will not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on your interests and you may participate in the decisions.

2. The facts indicate that the Cannabis-EIR decisions will affect a significant segment of the
businesses in the County, and do not appear to have a unique effect on your financial interests. You
may participate under the public generally exception on these decisions, to the extent that the
decisions do not have a unique effect on your financial interests.

3. No. The Cannabis Tax Initiative decision involves a specific tax rate applicable to your
type of business, and thus has a unique effect on your financial interests.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You were recently elected to the Trinity County Board of Supervisors. You are the single-
member of Brown's Lumber Co. LLC, which includes 640 acres of timberland and a permitted,
10,000 square foot cannabis grower operation. You note that the business is not currently showing a
profit due to expenses and that your clients are from outside the County. There is no current
cannabis transportation, retail or distribution business operating in the County. As a result, you state
that cannabis grown in your county is a commodity with state-wide competition, rather than in-
county competition. Following a discussion regarding your initial request, you identified three
decisions that will come before the Board that touch on cannabis industry issues.

The rezone parcels are located in the Douglas City area, over 50 miles from your real
property. The applicant requests to rezone four parcels along the highway from "Highway
Commercial" to "Heavy Commercial" so that it may renovate an existing building into a
commercial cannabis storage and distribution facility.² You state it is unlikely that this decision will
impact your business, as you understand the services the applicant plans to offer are things you can
already do on your premises with the correct permit.

Trinity County is proposing to amend and readopt its existing regulations related to the
licensing of commercial cannabis operations (the "County Cannabis Program"). In support of its
proposal, the County has conducted an environmental review of the impacts of implementing the
County Cannabis Program. The draft Cannabis-EIR provides an analysis and mitigation methods to
address noted environmental impacts of the Cannabis Program. Options before the County include
accepting the draft EIR, incorporating the proposed mitigation measures into the ordinances and
readopting the existing license program, decreasing permits in particular watersheds, increasing the
designated area for cultivation activities, and possibly rejecting the draft-EIR.

The County estimates that there are 3800 grow sites in the County; however, many of those
sites are not operating within the County Cannabis Program. There are currently 330 cannabis
business permits issued by the County, with a possible 500 permits available for issue. You state

² According to the draft Cannabis-EIR, there are currently 10 state-licensed distribution facilities in the county
as of 2018.

File No. A-19-212
Page No. 3

that while the Cannabis-EIR findings affect the County Cannabis Program, it does not affect your
LLC's specific permit type or zoning. In a follow-up email, you clarified that the California
Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) requirements are through the state, not the County. You
understand that the mitigations the EIR poses are essentially already required and have been
identified or implemented through the multiple agencies that oversee the cannabis licensing
program. In relationship to your specific permit type and property zoning, you don't see the EIR
affecting you beyond that of current CEQA requirements. Your property is not located in one of the
watershed areas primarily at issue. You anticipate that the Cannabis-EIR decision would not change
your current eligibility, future expansion opportunity, nor add additional costs to the business's
operations. You note that the EIR may include a limit on the number of licenses issued in the
County, however, you note that this number is dependent on a statewide program.

A cannabis tax initiative will be before the Board for a vote to place the measure on the
March ballot. The tax would apply to cultivation, retail, distribution and manufacturing of cannabis.
It proposes a flat square footage tax on cultivators, with separate tiers for three different types of
cultivation: outdoors, mixed-light and indoor at rates of $.85, 1.15 and 1.45 per square foot of
cultivation, respectively. Cannabis operations in distribution and manufacturing would be taxed on
a percentage of gross receipts. You wish to add language to the proposed measure to sunset the tax
in two years and allow for a committee to analyze tax rate options relating to square footage, gross
receipts or pounds of production.

According to the draft Cannabis-EIR, there are 111 state-licensed outdoor cultivation sites,
85 mixed-light and 2 indoor sites in the County as of 2018. You state that the majority of cultivators
in the County have a 10,000 square foot grow. You would fall in the tier for mixed-light growers at
a rate of $1.15 per square foot.

ANALYSIS

The Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of
the public official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Section 87103 defines
financial interests to include:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management
(Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(b)), including a pro rata share of interests in real
property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate

File No. A-19-212
Page No. 4

family owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater
(Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87103(c)) including any community property interest in the income of a spouse
and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the
official (or his or her spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-
percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)).

Additionally, "income," other than a gift, does not include income received from any source
outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do business
within the jurisdiction, or not having done business within the jurisdiction during the two years
prior to the time of the decision. (Section 82030(a).) As a County Supervisor, your jurisdiction is
the County.

You have identified a business entity interest, real property interest and source of income
interest in Brown's Lumber Co, LLC. Please note that while you have not identified any clients that
meet the "source of income" definition, you have an interest in any clients of this LLC, as a source
of income, to the extent that your pro rata share of income exceeds $500 or more in the 12 months
prior to a decision. To the extent the decision may implicate a client of your business, you may wish
to seek additional advice.

Rezoning to allow Cannabis Distribution Facility

Foreseeability

Your financial interests are not the subject of the parcel(s) rezoning decision. The applicable
foreseeability standard for a financial interest not explicitly involved in a decision is whether the
financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.
(Regulation 18701(b).) If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances
not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Materiality- Real Property

Regulation 18702.2(b) states that the financial effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of
real property in which an official has a financial interest involving property 1,000 feet or more from the
property line of the official's property is presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted
with clear and convincing evidence the governmental decision would have a substantial effect on the
official's property. Your property is over 1,000 feet from the parcels at issue, and none of your facts
rebut the presumption.

Materiality - Business Entity and Source of Income

Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standard applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's business interest. Where the source of income is a

File No. A-19-212
Page No. 5

business entity, we also look to the materiality standard set forth in Regulation 18702.1. (Regulation
18702.3(a)(4).)

Where your business entity is not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation
18702.1(a)(2) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
business interest is material if any of the following criteria are met:

• The decision may result in an increase or decrease of the business's annual gross
revenues, or the value of the business's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to
or more than $1,000,000, or five percent of the business's annual gross revenues
and at least $10,000. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(2).)

• The decision may cause the business to incur or avoid additional expenses, or to
reduce or eliminate expenses, in an amount equal to or more than $250,000, or
one percent of the business's annual gross revenues and at least $2,500.
(Regulation 18702.1(a)(3).)

A decision relating to the zoning amendment would have a reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on your business entity if it would increase or decrease your business's annual gross
revenues, or the value of its assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $10,000. The
decision would also be "material" if it would cause your business to incur or avoid additional
expenses, or to reduce or eliminate expenses, in an amount equal to or more than $2,500.

You do not anticipate using the proposed facility for your business because you can already
engage in these activities on your property with the necessary permit, and you do not anticipate any
impact on your business related to the rezoning decision. Based upon these facts, it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the proposed rezoning decision would have a material financial effect
on your business and source of income interest.

County Cannabis-FIR and Cannabis Tax Initiative

Decisions regarding the draft Cannabis EIR and the County Cannabis Program, as well as
the Cannabis Tax Initiative will potentially impact the entire cannabis industry in the County. The
applicable foreseeability standard for a financial interest not explicitly involved in a decision of
general application is whether the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and
more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) If the financial result cannot be
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.

Materiality - Business Entity and Source of Income

The above standard set forth in Regulation 18702.1 is applicable to these decisions as well.
You would have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in the decision if it would
increase or decrease your business's annual gross revenues, or the value of its assets or liabilities, in
an amount equal to or more than $10,000. The decision would also be "material" if it would cause
your business to incur or avoid additional expenses, or to reduce or eliminate expenses, in an
amount equal to or more than $2,500.

File No. A-19-212
Page No. 6

As noted above, you do not anticipate the County's Cannabis-EIR decisions will affect your
specific permit type or zoning. You anticipate that the Cannabis-EIR decision would not change
your current eligibility, future expansion opportunity, gross revenues, value of assets or liabilities,
nor add additional costs to the business's operations. However, where the decisions are far-
reaching, and impact the entire County Cannabis Program it is likely that the above standard may be
met.

Applying the above standard in Regulation 18702.1 to the Cannabis Tax Initiative decisions,
you would have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in the decision, because it
would cause your business to incur or avoid tax expenses of more than $2,500.

Public Generally

A public official that has a reasonably foreseeable and material financial interest in a
decision may still participate if the official demonstrates that the financial effect is (1) not unique to
the official and (2) shared by a significant segment of the jurisdiction. (Section 87103, Regulation
18703.)

Regulation 18703 defines a unique effect on the official's financial interest(s) to include a
disproportionate effect on any of the following:

(1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity.

(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity of a
project that is the subject of a decision.

(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the
cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest.

(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the
official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a
decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage.

(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the
person is a source of income or gifts to the official.

(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family.

A significant segment of the public includes at least 25 percent of: all businesses within the
official's jurisdiction; or all real property, commercial real property, or residential real property; or
all individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).)

Decisions relating to the draft EIR and County Cannabis Program will broadly impact all
cannabis businesses in the County. The Cannabis Tax Initiative decision will cause all 330 permit

File No. A-19-212
Page No. 7

holders to incur or avoid tax expenses. You were able to ascertain from the County Assessor's
Office there are 1,000 businesses 1,000 businesses that the assessor's office assesses for business
property taxes in the County. Therefore, a significant segment, at least 25 percent of all businesses
in the County will be affected by the decisions related to the draft EIR and the County Cannabis
Program, as well as by the Cannabis Tax Initiative. The facts do not indicate that your business will
be uniquely affected by the draft EIR decisions as compared to the significant segment, and the
public generally exception applies. We caution that where a decision does uniquely impact your
financial interests, or any of the above listed factors, the public generally exception will not apply.

For the Cannabis Tax Initiative decision, the public generally exception does not apply. The
Cannabis Tax Initiative will uniquely affect your business, because it proposes a tax rate specific to
your type of mixed-light cultivation business, and distinct rates for other types of cannabis
businesses. Your financial interests will be uniquely affected, and you may not participate in this
decision.

Appearances not Prohibited

You also inquired as to the procedure for addressing a matter before the Board where you
have a conflict of interest, and you wish to speak as a member of the public. As a general matter,
Regulation 18704(d)(2) permits an appearance by the official as a member of the general public
before an agency in its regular decision-making capacity if the appearance is on matters related
solely to the official's personal interests, including interests in:

(A) Real property owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate
family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family;

(B) A business entity owned entirely by the official, members of his or her
immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family; or

(C) A business entity over which the official, members of his or her immediate
family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family solely or
jointly exercise full direction and control.

This is a narrow exception and allows only for public comments regarding the implications
of the decisions on your interest. The exception does not allow presentations of a general nature
where you have a conflict of interest regarding the decision.

Segmentation of a Decision

Regulation 18706 permits an agency to segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest to allow participation by the official, provided the following conditions are
satisfied:

(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

File No. A-19-212
Page No. 8

(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
the other decisions;

(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's
participation in any way; and

(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified.

We advise you generally that the County is permitted to segment decisions to facilitate
participation by an official who would otherwise be disqualified, in accordance with the above
requirements. You may wish to seek additional advice before taking part in a segmented decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: L. Karen Harrison
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:aja

( 4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the 
official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a 
decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the 
person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family. 
A significant segment of the public includes at least 25 percent of: all businesses within the 
official's jurisdiction; or all real property, commercial real property, or residential real property; or 
all individuals within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) 
Decisions relating to the draft EIR and County Cannabis Program will broadly impact all 
cannabis businesses in the County. The Cannabis Tax Initiative decision will cause all 330 permit  
holders to incur or avoid tax expenses. You were able to ascertain from the County Assessor's 
Office there are 1,000 businesses 1,000 businesses that the assessor's office assesses for business 
property taxes in the County. Therefore, a significant segment, at least 25 percent of all businesses 
in the County will be affected by the decisions related to the draft EIR and the County Cannabis 
Program, as well as by the Cannabis Tax Initiative. The facts do not indicate that your business will 
be uniquely affected by the draft EIR decisions as compared to the significant segment, and the 
public generally exception applies. We caution that where a decision does uniquely impact your 
financial interests, or any of the above listed factors, the public generally exception will not apply. 
For the Cannabis Tax Initiative decision, the public generally exception does not apply. The 
Cannabis Tax Initiative will uniquely affect your business, because it proposes a tax rate specific to 
your type of mixed-light cultivation business, and distinct rates for other types of cannabis 
businesses. Your financial interests will be uniquely affected, and you may not participate in this 
decision. 
Appearances not Prohibited 
You also inquired as to the procedure for addressing a matter before the Board where you 
have a conflict of interest, and you wish to speak as a member of the public. As a general matter, 
Regulation 18704( d)(2) permits an appearance by the official as a member of the general public 
before an agency in its regular decision-making capacity if the appearance is on matters related 
solely to the official's personal interests, including interests in: 
(A) Real property owned entirely by the official, members of his or her immediate 
family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family; 
(B) A business entity owned entfrely by the official, members of his or her 
immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family; or 
(C) A business entity over which the official, members of his or her immediate 
family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family solely or 
jointly exercise full direction and control. 
This is a narrow exception and allows only for public comments regarding the implications 
of the decisions on your interest. The exception does not allow presentations of a general nature· 
where you have a conflict of interest regarding the decision. 
Segmentation of a Decision 
Regulation 18706 permits an agency to segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest to allow participation by the official, provided the following conditions are 
satisfied: 
· 
· 
(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not "inextricably interrelated" to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;  
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
(4) Once the decision ir:i which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or 
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified. 
We advise you generally that the County is permitted to segment decisions to facilitate 
participation by an official who would otherwise be disqualified, in accordance with the above 
requirements. You may wish to seek additional advice before taking part in a segmented decision.

QUESTION: As a member of the Trinity County Board of Supervisors and the sole member of the limited 
liability company established for your cannabis grower business, may you participate in the 
following decisions: 
1. An application to rezone four highway commercial parcels to allow a commercial 
cannabis storage and distribution facility. 
2. The draft Environmental Impact Report specific to the County Cannabis Program 
("Cannabis-EIR") 
3. A cannabis tax initiative approval before the Board applicable to cultivation, retail, 
distribution and manufacturing. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. The rezone application will not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial 
effect on your interests and you may participate in the decisions. 
2. The facts indicate that the Cannabis-BIR decisions will affect a significant segment of.the 
businesses in the County, and do not appear to have a unique effect on your financial interests. You 
may participate under the public generally exception on these decisions, to the extent that the 
decisions do not have a unique effect on your financial interests. 
3. No. The Cannabis Tax Initiative decision involves a specific tax rate applicable to your 
type of business, and thus has a unique effect on your financial interests.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 November 19, 2019 Jeremy Brown P.O. Box 253 Burnt Ranch, Ca 95573 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-212 Dear Mr. Brown: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION As a member of the Trinity County Board of Supervisors and the sole member of the limited liability company established for your cannabis grower business, may you participate in the following decisions: 1. An application to rezone four highway commercial parcels to allow a commercial cannabis storage and distribution facility. 2. The draft Environmental Impact Report specific to the County Cannabis Program ("Cannabis-EIR") 3. A cannabis tax initiative approval before the Board applicable to cultivation, retail, distribution and manufacturing. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-212 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION 1. Yes. The rezone application will not have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your interests and you may participate in the decisions. 2. The facts indicate that the Cannabis-EIR decisions will affect a significant segment of the businesses in the County, and do not appear to have a unique effect on your financial interests. You may participate under the public generally exception on these decisions, to the extent that the decisions do not have a unique effect on your financial interests. 3. No. The Cannabis Tax Initiative decision involves a specific tax rate applicable to your type of business, and thus has a unique effect on your financial interests. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER You were recently elected to the Trinity County Board of Supervisors. You are the single- member of Brown's Lumber Co. LLC, which includes 640 acres of timberland and a permitted, 10,000 square foot cannabis grower operation. You note that the business is not currently showing a profit due to expenses and that your clients are from outside the County. There is no current cannabis transportation, retail or distribution
